conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

Do you have any children under the age of 16, or aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have one child – my son Alex. He's 19 years old and he's currently studying for his A-levels at sixth form college. He lives at home with me here in Bristol.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. It's helpful to know about Alex and his situation.

To continue assessing your eligibility, I need to ask: Are you responsible for Alex? This typically means either you live with him (which you've mentioned you do) or you're contributing at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards his care.

Also, could you confirm - is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, I'm responsible for him – he lives with me at home, and I cover all his expenses like food, clothes, and things for college.

And no, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. It's just me looking after him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment:

1. Is Alex still in approved education (his A-levels at sixth form college)? Has he left education at any point, or is he continuously enrolled?

2. Does Alex work 24 hours or more per week, or does he receive any benefits in his own right such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, Alex is still enrolled in his A-levels – he's been there continuously, hasn't left or taken any breaks.

And no, he doesn't work full-time or anything like that. He focuses on his studies. He doesn't receive any benefits in his own name either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I have everything I need to assess your eligibility.

Based on what you've told me and the official Child Benefit rules, let me provide you with my decision:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:
- Alex is 19 years old and studying for his A-levels at sixth form college, which counts as approved education
- Child Benefit can continue for children under 20 who stay in approved education or training
- You are responsible for Alex - he lives with you and you cover all his expenses
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him
- Alex doesn't work 24+ hours per week or claim benefits in his own right, which would disqualify the claim

You should be able to claim Child Benefit for Alex and continue receiving it until he finishes his approved education, turns 20, or his circumstances change (such as starting full-time work or claiming certain benefits).

**Please note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, though you can still claim the benefit.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 19 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old and is currently studying for his A-levels at sixth form college, which qualifies as approved education. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for children under 16, but eligibility extends to children under 20 if they stay in approved education or training. You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you at home and you cover all his expenses (food, clothes, college supplies). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex does not work 24 hours or more per week and does not receive benefits in his own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit), which would otherwise disqualify the claim. Alex has been continuously enrolled in his A-level studies without breaks. Therefore, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for one child, Alex (19 years old), who lives with them in Bristol. Alex is in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college) and does not work full-time or claim benefits in his own right. The claimant covers all of Alex's expenses and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. All eligibility criteria are met.

Under UK Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for children under 16, but eligibility extends to children under 20 if they stay in approved education or training. You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you at home and you cover all his expenses (food, clothes, college supplies). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex does not work 24 hours or more per week and does not receive benefits in his own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit), which would otherwise disqualify the claim. Alex has been continuously enrolled in his A-level studies without breaks. Therefore, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for one child, Alex (19 years old), who lives with them in Bristol. Alex is in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college) and does not work full-time or claim benefits in his own right. The claimant covers all of Alex's expenses and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. All eligibility criteria are met.
